perturbation.original_text: The WeUm ³Critical Energy Infrastructure Information´ oU ³CEII´ aV XVed in WhiV AgUeemenW UefeUV Wo Whe official definition provided in 18 C.F.R. § 388.113(c)(1). CEII shall be protected under the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The WeUm ³Critical Energy Infrastructure Information´ oU ³CEII´ aV XVed in WhiV AgUeemenW UefeUV Wo Whe definition determined at the Disclosing Party's sole discretion. CEII shall be protected under the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of CEII to be at the discretion of the disclosing party introduces ambiguity and contradicts the official regulatory definition.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 18 C.F.R. § 388.113(c)(1), which provides a specific definition for Critical Energy Infrastructure Information. By stating the definition is at the Disclosing Party's discretion, it allows for definitions inconsistent with the federal regulation, thus undermining the protection intended by the regulation.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the law by changing the definition of CEII. The original text clearly states that the definition of CEII refers to the official definition provided in 18 C.F.R. § 388.113(c)(1). The changed text contradicts this by stating the definition is at the Disclosing Party's discretion, which undermines the standardized definition and protection intended by the regulation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that Title 18 is authoritative, and the change allows for definitions inconsistent with federal regulation.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that, in complying with its obligations under this Agreement, it shall employ an enhanced standard of care in order to prevent the disclosure and to protect the confidentiality of the CEII.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that, in complying with its obligations under this Agreement, it shall employ a reasonable standard of care in order to prevent the disclosure and to protect the confidentiality of the CEII.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'enhanced standard of care' to 'reasonable standard of care' lowers the required level of protection, potentially conflicting with security requirements associated with CEII.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change weakens the security obligations around CEII, potentially running counter to the intent of 18 C.F.R. § 388.113, which implicitly requires a high degree of protection for sensitive infrastructure information. Using a 'reasonable standard' may not be sufficient to meet regulatory expectations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'enhanced standard of care' to 'reasonable standard of care' significantly weakens the required level of protection for CEII. The Law Explanation suggests this contradicts the intent of 18 C.F.R. § 388.113, which implicitly requires a high degree of protection for sensitive infrastructure information. 'Reasonable standard' might not meet regulatory expectations, as a higher standard might be reasonably expected. Snippet 1 references 18 CFR 388.113 and its relation to Critical Energy/Electric Infrastructure Information (CEII) reinforcing the importance of this regulation and the seriousness of weakening language around the protection of CEII. This creates a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction because the original term has a specific legal meaning while the changed term does not, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may disclose CEII (i) only to its Representatives who have properly executed individual non-disclosure or confidentiality agreements in the course of their employment specifically pertaining to confidential information and CEII they receive in the course of their employment and (ii) only after informing each Representative of the restrictions in this Agreement on the disclosure and use of the CEII and that he or she must comply with such restrictions.. Further, such disclosure must be limited to the extent that it is reasonably necessary to evaluate the subject matter of the requested materials. The Receiving Party agrees to take all reasonable steps to cause its Representatives to comply with the terms of this Agreement and to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any Representative. The Receiving Party shall not disclose any CEII to any of its Representatives who have been convicted of any felony.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may disclose CEII (i) to its Representatives after informing each Representative of the restrictions in this Agreement on the disclosure and use of the CEII and that he or she must comply with such restrictions.. Further, such disclosure must be limited to the extent that it is reasonably necessary to evaluate the subject matter of the requested materials. The Receiving Party agrees to take all reasonable steps to cause its Representatives to comply with the terms of this Agreement and to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any Representative.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the need for individual non-disclosure agreements before disclosing CEII to representatives and allowing CEII disclosure to convicted felons decreases CEII protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification loosens the restrictions on who can access CEII, going against the implicit security requirements outlined in 18 C.F.R. § 388.113. The original text prohibited CEII disclosure to convicted felons and those without non-disclosure agreements; removing these restrictions reduces the protection of critical infrastructure information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for individual non-disclosure agreements and allows CEII disclosure to convicted felons. The Law Explanation states this goes against the implicit security requirements outlined in 18 C.F.R. § 388.113. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies 18 CFR 388.113 as relating to Critical Energy/Electric Infrastructure Information (CEII). By removing restrictions on who can access CEII, the modified contract weakens protections for critical infrastructure information, creating a contradiction because it permits actions that undermine the security implicitly required for CEII.

perturbation.original_text: Any action arising out of the performance of this Agreement must be filed and resolved exclusively in a state or federal court or tribunal sitting in the State of South Carolina. The parties consent and submit to the jurisdiction and venue of those courts and tribunals.
perturbation.changed_text: Any action arising out of the performance of this Agreement may be resolved through binding arbitration in a location agreed upon by both parties. The parties consent to participate in such arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the dispute resolution from courts in South Carolina to binding arbitration potentially limits legal recourse and contradicts the specified governing law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South Carolina Uniform Arbitration Act, S.C. Code Ann. § 15-48-10
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification attempts to circumvent the specified governing law (South Carolina) by mandating binding arbitration, which may not fully align with the legal standards and protections afforded under South Carolina law. While arbitration is permitted, forcing it may conflict with the intent of resolving disputes under the state's judicial system, particularly if it undermines statutory rights or remedies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Code of Laws - Title 15 - Chapter 48 - Uniform Arbitration Act Your browser does not support JavaScript! This page will not render correctly. Home Senate House Committee Postings and Reports Legislative Council Citizens' Interest Publications Staff Portal Chamber Video Senate (Audio Only) House (Audio Only) Video Schedule Video Archives How do I... Quick Search Search for: Full Site Search Bill Number Legislation Budget Code of Laws Code of Regulations Constitution House Journals Senate Journals LSA Bill Summary Find Your Legislators Contact Your Legislator Legislation Track Legislation Multi-Criteria Search Research South Carolina Law Ratifications & Acts Code of Laws Code of Regulations Constitution State Register Legislative Manual Purchase Manual View Manual Online Student Connection Visiting the State House Archives State Agency Websites Media Links South Carolina Law > Code of Laws > Title 15 South Carolina Code of Laws Unannotated Title 15 - Civil Remedies and Procedures CHAPTER 48 Uniform Arbitration Act SECTION 15-48-10. Validity of arbitration agreement; exceptions from operation of chapter. (a) A written agreement to submit any existing controversy to arbitration or a provision in a written contract to submit to arbitration any controversy thereafter arising between the parties is valid, enforceable and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. Notice that a contract is subject to arbitration pursuant to this chapter shall be typed in underlined capital letters, or rubber-stamped prominently, on the first page of the contract and unless such notice is displayed thereon the contract shall not be subject to arbitration. (b) This chapter however shall not apply to: (1) Any agreement or provision to arbitrate in which it is stipulated that this chapter shall not apply or to any arbitration or award thereunder; (2) Arbitration agreements between employers and employees or between their respective representatives unless the agreement provides that this chapter shall apply; provided, however, that notwithstanding any other provision of law, employers and employees or their respective representatives may not agree that workmen's compensation claims, unemployment compensation claims and collective bargaining disputes shall be subject to the provisions of this chapter and any such provision so agreed upon shall be null and void. An agreement to apply this chapter shall not be made a condition of employment. (3) A pre-agreement entered into when the relationship of the contracting parties is such that of lawyer-client or doctor-patient and the term "doctor" shall include all those persons licensed to practice medicine pursuant to Chapters 9, 15, 31, 37, 47, 51, 55, 67 and 69 of Title 40 of the 1976 Code. (4) Any claim arising out of personal injury, based on contract or tort, or to any insured or beneficiary under any insurance policy or annuity contract. HISTORY: 1978 Act No. 492, Section 1. SECTION 15-48-20. Proceedings to compel
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies dispute resolution in South Carolina courts, while the changed text mandates binding arbitration. This contradicts the original agreement to resolve disputes within the South Carolina judicial system. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Section 15-48-10(a) of the South Carolina Uniform Arbitration Act, validates arbitration agreements but doesn't override the initial agreement to court resolution. The change introduces a contradiction by potentially limiting legal recourse available under South Carolina law, as arbitration may not fully align with the state's legal standards and protections.
